pack_sources.author: AI-assisted implementation draft (unreviewed)

misconceptions.error_signature: Coefficients of x^2 and x are added as if their variable parts matched.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Sort terms by exponent before combining any coefficients.

misconceptions.description: Applies subtraction to only the first term.
misconceptions.error_signature: Later terms of the subtracted polynomial keep their original signs.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Distribute the negative sign to every term before collecting.

misconceptions.description: Omits one cross product when expanding.
misconceptions.error_signature: A product of two binomials contains fewer than four pre-combination products.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Use a two-by-two multiplication grid and fill every cell.

metaphors.description: Like powers are labeled drawers: coefficients can be combined only after every term is placed in the drawer carrying the same exponent.

error_patterns: polynomial_unlike_terms: combines coefficients across different exponents
polynomial_sign_loss: fails to negate every subtracted term
polynomial_missing_cross_product: omits a binomial cross product
sources: OpenStax Algebra and Trigonometry 2e, Section 1.5: Polynomials.

lesson_narrative.text: Polynomial operations preserve the power attached to each term while coefficients are added, subtracted, or produced by distribution.

lesson_narrative.spoken_text: The quantity two x plus three times x minus four expands to two x squared minus five x minus twelve.

lesson_narrative.text: A final scan by descending exponent makes missing powers and sign errors visible.

remediation.text: Write every polynomial in descending powers and insert zero placeholders for missing powers.

remediation.spoken_text: The quantity two x plus three times x minus four expands to two x squared minus five x minus twelve.

remediation.text: For a product, verify every term in the first factor multiplied every term in the second before collecting.

pack_sources.author: AI-assisted implementation draft (unreviewed)

misconceptions.description: Starts a special pattern before removing the greatest common monomial.
misconceptions.error_signature: The proposed factors retain a common coefficient or x power inside.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Remove the greatest common monomial before looking for another pattern.

misconceptions.description: Uses factors whose sum and product conditions are reversed.
misconceptions.error_signature: Proposed roots add to the constant and multiply to the x coefficient.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: For a monic quadratic, roots multiply to the constant and add to the opposite x coefficient.

misconceptions.description: Factors a difference of squares with equal signs.
misconceptions.error_signature: Both conjugate factors use plus or both use minus.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: A difference of squares requires one plus and one minus factor.

metaphors.description: Factoring is an audit trail run backward: remove what every term shares, then identify the multiplication pattern that reconstructs the original polynomial.

error_patterns: factoring_gcf_omitted: residual terms retain a common monomial
factoring_root_conditions_swapped: sum and product tests are reversed
factoring_conjugate_sign_error: difference-of-squares factors use matching signs
sources: OpenStax Intermediate Algebra 2e, Section 6.1: Greatest Common Factor and Factor by Grouping.
OpenStax Intermediate Algebra 2e, Section 6.4: General Strategy for Factoring Polynomials.

lesson_narrative.text: A normalized factorization begins with the greatest common monomial and leaves a primitive residual polynomial.

lesson_narrative.spoken_text: x squared minus nine equals x minus three times x plus three.

lesson_narrative.text: Multiplying the proposed factors is the final truth check; it must reproduce every original coefficient.

remediation.text: First name the common coefficient and lowest shared x power; divide them out term by term.

remediation.spoken_text: x squared minus nine equals x minus three times x plus three.

remediation.text: Then test the remaining pattern by multiplying the proposed factors back together.

pack_sources.author: AI-assisted implementation draft (unreviewed)

misconceptions.description: Changes only one side of an equation.
misconceptions.error_signature: A term is added, subtracted, multiplied, or divided on only one side.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Write the same balancing operation beside both sides before simplifying.

misconceptions.description: Moves a term by changing its sign without an explicit balancing operation.
misconceptions.error_signature: The collected equation has a sign inconsistent with subtracting the term from both sides.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Show the subtraction or addition on both sides rather than mentally moving a term.

misconceptions.description: Divides only one term by the final coefficient.
misconceptions.error_signature: The last division does not apply to the complete side of the equation.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: After collecting, divide both complete sides by the coefficient of x.

metaphors.description: An equation is a balanced scale: every operation is applied to both complete sides until one copy of x remains alone.

error_patterns: linear_unbalanced_operation: an inverse operation changes only one side
linear_collection_sign_error: a collected term has the wrong sign
linear_partial_division: final coefficient division applies to only one term
sources: OpenStax Elementary Algebra 2e, Section 2.2: Use a General Strategy to Solve Linear Equations.
OpenStax Intermediate Algebra 2e, Section 2.1: Solve Linear Equations.

lesson_narrative.text: Solving a linear equation preserves equality while variable terms are collected on one side and constants on the other.

lesson_narrative.spoken_text: Three x plus five equals x plus seventeen, then two x equals twelve, then x equals six.

lesson_narrative.text: The final substitution check must make the original left and right sides exactly equal.

remediation.text: Write one balancing operation per line and simplify both sides only after that operation is visible.

remediation.spoken_text: Three x plus five equals x plus seventeen, then two x equals twelve, then x equals six.

remediation.text: Substitute the candidate integer into the untouched original equation to catch sign or division errors.

pack_sources.author: AI-assisted implementation draft (unreviewed)

misconceptions.description: Attempts a zero-product step before the equation equals zero.
misconceptions.error_signature: Factors are set equal to zero while a nonzero expression remains on the other side.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Collect the entire equation on one side and write zero on the other before factoring.

misconceptions.description: Reports only one root from two distinct linear factors.
misconceptions.error_signature: The response contains a solution from only one factor.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Set every distinct factor equal to zero and collect all roots in one set.

misconceptions.description: Solves x-r=0 with the wrong sign.
misconceptions.error_signature: A factor x-r produces root -r rather than r.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Undo the signed constant in each linear factor with an explicit balancing step.

metaphors.description: A factorable quadratic opens two linear doors: after creating a zero product, each factor can independently make that product zero.

error_patterns: quadratic_zero_form_missing: zero-product reasoning begins before one side is zero
quadratic_root_omitted: only one distinct linear factor is solved
quadratic_factor_sign_reversed: a factor produces a root with the wrong sign
sources: OpenStax Intermediate Algebra 2e, Section 6.5: Solve Polynomial Equations by Factoring.
OpenStax College Algebra 2e, Section 2.5: Quadratic Equations.

lesson_narrative.text: Every reviewed pilot equation is monic and factors over integer roots, so the lesson stays within factoring and linear-equation reasoning.

lesson_narrative.spoken_text: x squared plus fifteen x plus fifty-six equals zero, factors as x plus seven times x plus eight, and has solution set negative eight and negative seven.

lesson_narrative.text: Repeated factors contribute one set member, while distinct factors contribute both roots.

remediation.text: Confirm the equation is zero, then find integer factor roots using the sum and product checks.

remediation.spoken_text: x squared plus fifteen x plus fifty-six equals zero, factors as x plus seven times x plus eight, and has solution set negative eight and negative seven.

remediation.text: Solve every factor, remove duplicate roots, and substitute each set member into the original equation.
